Hi @whzup , I understand your point about having the user check if he/she needs a static or dynamic topology. However, this requires a lot of mental processing on the user-side to differentiate between the two. If we can provide a reasonable default and it works, then it's all good. I still maintain that we set a default value for this parameter.

1. **On active choices**
> If we implement it without a default statement the user has actively to choose which topology he actually wants.

Default values provide a baseline from which a user can adapt his/her system: "If a `dynamic` topology didn't work, then maybe a `static` topology will." as opposed to asking "What's the difference between `dynamic` and `static`?" right off the bat. Sure, I can try one first randomly, but why not just set a default?

Our main philosophy is to keep things simple. We only expose the API *whenever needed*. 
The library requires a necessary input from the user, and it gives the user's expected output. All customization parameters should either be defaulted or unexposed. The `static=False` option is the most reasonable for most use-cases, so we set it that way.

We want to make the users familiar with the `input -> output` (or f(x) = y) scheme before introducing other parameters. Again, in this manner, the user checks if his system works given a baseline, then he/she adapts the algorithm until it clicks.

A notable example for this would be the [SVM classifier](http://scikit-learn.org/stable/modules/generated/sklearn.svm.SVC.html) of scikit-learn. Sensible defaults are given and it provides a baseline for experimentation (e.g. if RBF doesn't work, maybe a polynomial kernel will, etc.).

>  There are arguments for both: static uses less computation time and saves some resources on the user's side

I might need some supporting evidence for this aside from looking at the code. Static *may* be "faster," but is it significantly faster? If you can profile this that would be nice. 

2. **On readability**
>  By implementing it so the user has to choose whether the topology is static or dynamic he has more control over his code.

We have solved the "more control over his code" part when we implemented the parameter itself. Again, actively choosing something still requires a lot of thought. Let's give them a sensible default and have them iterate if it didn't work.

Say we have a `qs` where `sort_order` looks like this: `1, 2, 3, 4`.
We add a new item with `sort_order == 2`.
`Orderable._save` creates `to_shift` which looks like: `2, 3, 4`.
The old code then tried to call `to_shift.update(sort_order = F('sort_order') + 1)`.
This works fine without any `unique` constraints, but if `sort_order` is `unique_together` with another field (as in `SubTask`) the `update` will raise `IntegrityError` when it increments `2` to `3`. The internal state of `to_shift` is now `3, 3, 4`, which is invalid.

If Django (and the db) supported `DEFERRED`, we could defer the unique check until the `update` had completed (`3, 4, 5`) and everything would work as in the case without `unique`.

indexbytes from six in Python 3 is just `operator.getitem` (which is assume is just `[]`), in Python 2 it is:
```
def indexbytes(buf, i):
        return ord(buf[i])
```
Ref: https://github.com/django/django/blob/master/django/utils/six.py#L629

So before when it was a string it made sense to do `ord(buf[i])`. But now, it is `bytes`, so just using `[]` is enough, using indexbytes on Python 2 will break with:

```
ERROR: smarkets.tests.streaming_api.framing.test_frame_decode_all(bytearray(b'\x05abcde\x03abc'), (['abcde', 'abc'], ''))
----------------------------------------------------------------------
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/nose/case.py", line 197, in runTest
    self.test(*self.arg)
  File "/home/adam/workspace/smk_python_sdk/smarkets/tests/streaming_api/framing.py", line 111, in check_frame_decode_all
    eq_(frame_decode_all(byte_array), output)
  File "/home/adam/workspace/smk_python_sdk/smarkets/streaming_api/framing.py", line 57, in frame_decode_all
    decoded = uleb128_decode(to_decode)
  File "/home/adam/workspace/smk_python_sdk/smarkets/streaming_api/framing.py", line 90, in uleb128_decode
    current_byte = indexbytes(to_decode, position)
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/six.py", line 655, in indexbytes
    return ord(buf[i])
TypeError: ord() expected string of length 1, but int found
```

Because it is doing ord() on integer instead of string of length 1.

Unfortunately a space is the katcp delimiter, but a comma might do if we update the KATCP spec for 5.1 to exclude comma from sensor-names, as in practice I don't think we have any sensors with comma's in the name.

The katcp spec has the following for the message BNF:
```
<message> ::= <type> <name> [<msgid>] <arguments> <eol>
<type> ::= "?" | "!" | "#"
<name> ::= alpha (alpha | digit | "-")*
<msgid> ::= "[" (digit / "0") digit* "]"
<whitespace> ::= (space | tab) [<whitespace>]
<eol> ::= newline | carriage-return
<arguments> ::= (<whitespace> <argument> <arguments>) | <whitespace> | ""
<argument> ::= (<plain> | <escape>)+
<escape> ::= "\" <escapecode>
<escapecode> ::= "\" | "_" | zero | "n" | "r" | "e" | "t" | "@"
<special> ::= backslash | space | null | newline | carriage-return | escape | tab
<plain> ::= character / <special>
```

so, basically the question here is not about to correct for non-normal distribution or not (correction as it is does not depend on the distribution of the data), but whether to correct for the bootstrap or not. In our scenario it make sense to make the same correction of CIs as for the hypothesis testing, since here we report corrected confidence intervals and, basically, they should be approximately similar to CIs reported by hypothesis testing. Bootstrap is not a hypothesis testing though, but in our case it may be considered as another way of representing the data. Here we correct more in terms of the decision on how to select a confidence interval: the more kpis we observe the higher the chance that one kpi will give different results than others.  But if you have other ideas, I would be happy to discuss.

Oh. May be you didn't noticed, but recently we had changed how Patroni works with config files.
After initializing of new database it renames ``postgresql.conf`` into ``postgresql.base.conf``.
Later it writes into ``postgresql.conf`` something like:
```
include 'postgresql.base.conf'
param1 = 'value1'
param2 = 'value2'
etc...
```

In your case it will rename ``pipelinedb.conf`` into ``postgresql.base.conf``. It will not hurt, but doesn't look nice.

I would propose rename ``config`` into ``config_base_name`` and at the end to have something like:
```diff
@@ -59,8 +59,9 @@ class Postgresql(object):
         self.callback = config.get('callbacks') or {}
         self.use_slots = config.get('use_slots', True)
         self._schedule_load_slots = self.use_slots
-        self._postgresql_conf = os.path.join(self._data_dir, 'postgresql.conf')
-        self._postgresql_base_conf_name = 'postgresql.base.conf'
+        config_base_name = config.get('config_base_name', 'postgresql')
+        self._postgresql_conf = os.path.join(self._data_dir, config_base_name + '.conf')
+        self._postgresql_base_conf_name = config_base_name + '.base.conf'
         self._postgresql_base_conf = os.path.join(self._data_dir, self._postgresql_base_conf_name)
         self._recovery_conf = os.path.join(self._data_dir, 'recovery.conf')
         self._configuration_to_save = (self._postgresql_conf, self._postgresql_base_conf,
```

The get function from std.HTTPClient does not have a timeout parameter (https://github.com/cablehead/python-consul/blob/master/consul/std.py), so it'll always be None and the `if timeout is None` check is redundant. 

I think you should add comments explaining what you are doing here, how about:

    # Set a default timeout for the request.session.request function, that is used
    # internally  by the methods request.session.get, request.session.post and
    # others. We monkey-patch here to avoid overriding each individual method from
    # request.sesson.*. By default, the timeout is not set. It means that a new
    # session may hang almost indefinitely waiting for the server to respond,
    # which is not what we want in Patroni.

And for the get function:

    # The get function is overridden to handle a special case of it being called
    # with an index and wait parameters. That form indicates that a user needs to
    # wait for the given key to change its value, with a wait timeout supplied. We
    # don't want our monkey-patched timeout to be less than the value of the wait
    # parameter, therefore, we set it to either the value of wait or a default of 5 minutes.

Maybe a good solution is to assign different types of work that stress different types of resources. For example, one could assign several tasks that sync and publish repositories (as is done now), and one could also assign tasks that calculate applicability for each of those repositories. The sync/publish tasks stress the system's disk and the applicability calculations stress the system's CPU. This gives the test a greater ability to find and stress bottlenecks.

A danger with this approach is that one might start assigning work that can be consumed faster than it's being assigned, thus muting the effects of any bottlenecks found. For example, if applicability calculation is a multi-threaded process, then a good Xeon, i7, Epyc or Ryzen CPU might just blow right through those tasks. Hrmmm.
